summary: Gruber tears apart Real Networks CEO Rob Glaser's interview in The Guardian, where Glaser dismisses Apple as a competitor while positioning Microsoft as Real's only rival. Gruber highlights how Glaser's strategy amounts to waiting for non-Apple portable devices to become compelling, which only gives Apple more time to dominate. He catches Glaser contradicting himself — admitting iPods drive music sales while blaming the music industry for allowing non-interoperable devices. Gruber is particularly scathing about Glaser's claim that piracy is the only way to get interoperable music, pointing out DRM-free stores like eMusic exist. The piece exposes Glaser's talking points as recycled Microsoft propaganda and his business strategy as wishful inaction.
key_points: Glaser conspicuously avoids acknowledging Apple as a competitor, referring only to Microsoft as a rival despite Apple's 65% European download market share
Real's stated strategy of waiting for better non-Apple portable devices is self-defeating — every year of waiting lets Apple sell more iPods and lock in more customers
Glaser contradicts himself by admitting iPods drive iTunes sales while arguing the music industry erred by allowing non-interoperable devices
The claim that piracy is the only way to get interoperable music is false — DRM-free stores like eMusic already existed
Accusing prospective customers of being thieves is borrowed from Microsoft's playbook and is unlikely to be an effective business strategy
money_quotes: I'm always left with the impression that he's either a moron or a liar. (My money is on liar.)

I'm willing to cut him some slack because it's his job as CEO to be an advocate for his company and its shareholders. I certainly don't expect him to stand up and admit that Apple has them licked. But that's no justification for just making shit up.
Translation: 'We're fucked, because there aren't any cool gadgets that play our DRM-protected audio formats.'
Just keep waiting. The longer Real waits, the more iPods Apple sells, and, uh, somehow that'll make it easier to succeed at some unspecified point in the future.
And Real's core business is what? 'Waiting'?
The music industry has had no role in the success of the iPod.
And these so-called 'friends' are probably shoplifting their CDs, right?
I'm intrigued by Glaser's strategy of accusing millions of prospective customers of being dishonest thieves.
key_insight: Rob Glaser's strategy of ignoring Apple, waiting indefinitely, and parroting Microsoft talking points about piracy reveals a CEO who has already lost but won't admit it.
